title: Polymer Clay Sculpted Snail Shell Pendant

glossaryTerms.definition: A hand-building method where clay is rolled into long ropes and then coiled or stacked to build up a form.
The process of hardening polymer clay by baking it in a domestic oven at the temperature specified by the manufacturer, typically 110-130 degrees C.
A shape that winds continuously around a central axis while moving outward, as in a shell or coil.

content.text: A snail shell pendant is built from a single clay coil that spirals down onto itself, a form of
coil building
adapted for polymer clay. The
spiral form
is inherently stable: the coils press against each other as you build, holding the shape without any armature. The ridge detail that defines the shell is added after the base form is in place. After
curing
, the pendant can be strung on cord or chain. It is a decorative piece only.

materials.name: Polymer clay, contrasting colour for ridge detail
Jump ring and necklace cord or thin leather cord

tools.name: Pottery rolling pin or smooth glass bottle for rolling coils
Baking tray lined with greaseproof paper

content.text: Preparation

Warm all clay in your hands until fully pliable. Cold clay is prone to cracking as you roll the coil. Roll a test coil from a small piece first to check the clay is warm enough: if it cracks along the outside as you roll, work it in your hands for another minute before proceeding. Preheat the oven to 110-130 degrees C.

Roll the main clay into a long coil approximately 6-7 mm in diameter and about 18-20 cm long. Use the flat of your palm and the work surface, rolling with even pressure from the centre outward, not just from one end. The coil should taper slightly at one end; this end will become the tip of the shell.

Start the spiral from the wider end of the coil. Curl the thick end into a tight circle about 8 mm across. Press the second ring against the first firmly. Continue winding outward in a flat spiral, pressing each new ring firmly against the previous one as you go.

After four or five rings, begin angling the coil slightly upward and inward to give the shell a domed rather than flat profile. Imagine the spiral rising toward a central point from a flat base. The tapered end of the coil should finish at the top-centre of the dome.

Press the tip firmly into the top of the dome so it does not protrude. Smooth the join with your fingertip. The outer surface of the shell should show the coil ridges clearly as a series of raised rings winding inward.

If using contrasting ridge detail, roll a very fine coil (about 2 mm diameter) of the second colour and press it into the groove between each main coil ring. This picks out the spiral lines and gives the surface more definition. Press gently so the accent coil sits inside the groove without flattening.

Use the needle tool to pierce a hanging hole through the top of the shell, passing through from front to back just above the coil structure. Push the needle straight through and wriggle it slightly to create a hole large enough for a jump ring, about 2 mm in diameter.
Place the shell face-up on the lined baking tray and bake at 110-130 degrees C for 20-25 minutes. Cool in the oven with the door ajar.
Once fully cool, thread the jump ring through the hanging hole and close it. Attach the cord or chain through the jump ring. The shell pendant is ready to wear.
Variations
Blend two colours in the main coil for a natural streaked shell effect.
Make a matching set of three shells in different sizes from the same clay colour for a layered pendant necklace.
After curing, sand lightly with 400-grit wet-and-dry paper and apply a coat of varnish for a polished ceramic look.
Use metallic-finish clay for the main shell and a darker accent colour for the ridges to suggest an aged bronze effect.

items.problem: Coil cracks as it is rolled
items.cause: Clay too cold or not adequately conditioned before rolling
items.fix: Warm the clay in your hands for an additional 2-3 minutes and fold and re-roll several times before starting the coil.

items.problem: Rings of the spiral separate during baking
items.cause: Individual coil rings not pressed firmly enough together before baking
items.fix: Press each new ring firmly against the previous one as you build. Run your finger along the join after each ring to ensure full contact across the whole width.

items.problem: Hanging hole closes up during baking
items.cause: Hole too small or clay softened around it during heating
items.fix: Pierce the hole slightly larger than you think necessary before baking. After curing, enlarge a closed hole by gently reheating the piece for 5 minutes, then re-piercing while warm.
